summary: Dan Luu demonstrates that while web bloat was previously constrained by slow internet connections, CPU performance for web apps hasn't scaled as quickly as bandwidth, making modern websites increasingly inaccessible to users with low-end devices even with fast internet. Testing popular platforms on devices like the Tecno Spark 8C and Itel P32 reveals that newer platforms (Discourse, Medium, Substack) can be 20-30x slower than older alternatives (MyBB, old WordPress), with many modern sites being completely unusable or crashing browsers on budget devices. The article challenges the widespread developer attitude that CPU speed is "infinite" and that only wealthy users with premium devices matter, noting that globally accessible design remains achievable as demonstrated by older, simpler platforms.
key_points: Modern websites are becoming inaccessible to people with low-end devices despite having high-speed connections, as CPU performance hasn't scaled as quickly as bandwidth
Testing shows older platforms (MyBB, phpBB, old WordPress) are 3-33x faster than modern alternatives (Discourse, Medium, Substack) on low-end devices
Many developers assume CPU speed is infinite and optimize for metrics like LCP rather than actual usability, often gaming metrics without improving user experience
There's a prevalent attitude among developers that non-wealthy users don't matter, despite billions of people worldwide using low-end devices
Simple, static pages with full content loading work far better on low-end devices than modern dynamic loading approaches
money_quotes: In the case of Discourse, a hardware engineer is an embarrassment not deserving of a job if they can't hit 90% of the performance of an all-time-great performance team but, as a software engineer, delivering 3% the performance of a non-highly-optimized application like MyBB is no problem.

It's fairly common for programmers to expect that hardware will solve all their problems, and then, when that doesn't happen, pass the issue onto the user, explaining why the programmer needn't do anything to help the user.
When asked if 100% of users are on iOS, the founder of Discourse says 'The influential users who spend money tend to be, I'll tell you that'.
Many sites that use modern techniques like partially loading the page and then dynamically loading the rest of it tend to be less usable than the scores in the table indicate.

It's still the case that many users don't have broadband speeds, both inside and outside of the U.S. and that much of the modern web isn't usable for people with slow internet, but the exponential increase in bandwidth has outpaced web bloat for typical sites.
key_insight: Developer assumptions that CPU speed is infinite and that only wealthy users matter have created a web where modern platforms can be orders of magnitude slower than decade-old alternatives, making much of the internet unusable for billions of people with low-end devices despite these devices being powerful enough to run 3D games and compile complex codebases.
